title: AI Gateways: Locking Down Model Access

modules.title: What AI Gateways Do and Why Uncontrolled Model Access Is Dangerous
modules.sub: Understanding attack surfaces created by exposed AI model endpoints
modules.description: Learners map the threat landscape around unprotected AI APIs, identifying how direct model access enables prompt injection, data exfiltration, and capability abuse. They analyze real-world scenarios where missing gateway controls led to security incidents. By the end, learners can articulate the security case for placing a gateway layer between clients and production AI models.

modules.title: Authentication, Rate Limiting, and Prompt Inspection at the Gateway Layer
modules.sub: Designing controls that enforce least-privilege access to AI models
modules.description: Learners examine the three core control categories a gateway must enforce: identity-based authentication, rate and quota policies, and content-level prompt inspection rules. They evaluate policy configurations for common weaknesses such as overly broad API key scopes, missing per-user throttles, and inspection rule gaps. Learners leave able to specify a baseline control set for a production AI gateway deployment.

modules.title: Tool Lab: Configuring and Auditing an AI API Gateway
modules.sub: Hands-on gateway setup, rule authoring, and security audit walkthrough
modules.description: In a guided lab environment, learners configure authentication schemes, write prompt inspection rules, and apply rate-limiting policies on a representative AI API gateway. They then perform a structured audit of their configuration, using a checklist to surface misconfigurations and policy gaps. The lab produces a hardened gateway configuration and a completed audit report learners can reference in real deployments.

modules.title: Detecting Data Exfiltration Through Model Interfaces
modules.sub: Recognizing and alerting on sensitive data leaving through AI responses
modules.description: Learners study the patterns attackers use to extract sensitive data through model outputs, including indirect prompt injection and response harvesting techniques. They configure detection rules and log analysis queries to surface anomalous output behavior at the gateway layer. By the end, learners can define alerting criteria and response workflows for suspected AI-channel data exfiltration.

modules.title: Governance Policies for Enterprise AI Gateway Deployment
modules.sub: Building policy frameworks that keep AI access controlled and auditable
modules.description: Learners draft governance artifacts including access control policies, approved model registries, and audit logging requirements needed to operationalize an enterprise AI gateway program. They evaluate how gateway controls align with existing compliance frameworks such as zero-trust architecture and data residency requirements. The module closes with learners producing a governance policy template ready for stakeholder review.

synopsis: Every unguarded API call to a production AI model is a potential data exfiltration vector, a prompt injection surface, or an unauthorized capability exposure waiting to be exploited. This course teaches security and DevSecOps professionals to design, configure, and audit AI gateway controls that enforce least-privilege access to models in enterprise environments.
rationale: Organizations are deploying AI APIs into production at speed while gateway-layer security controls — the critical enforcement point between users and models — remain poorly understood and inconsistently implemented.
learning_outcome: Learners can deploy and audit an AI API gateway with enforced authentication, prompt inspection rules, and rate-limiting policies that prevent unauthorized model access and data leakage.
source_signals: Show HN: GoModel – an open-source AI gateway in Go
Keeper Security: The identity security platform for humans, machines, and AI agents
